description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization
teaching_value: Gives students a high-level mental map of the entire RLHF pipeline before diving into details, showing how each phase builds on the previous one and where RLHF fits relative to pretraining and SFT
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
suggested_caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)

description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of token-by-token language model completion concrete and intuitive, helping students viscerally understand how LLMs generate text before learning how RLHF modifies that behavior
when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
suggested_caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens
teaching_value: Concretizes the scale of pretraining data (1T tokens) and illustrates the diversity of data sources used, helping students understand the data bottleneck problem and why data sourcing decisions matter for model behavior
when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
suggested_caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)

alt: We're at the risk of running out of Internet data
description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the looming data scarcity problem for LLM pretraining, motivating why data efficiency and alternative training signals (like human feedback) are increasingly important research directions
when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
suggested_caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)

description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT
teaching_value: Concretely shows why raw pretraining completion is insufficient for user-facing applications — the model may produce valid but unhelpful completions — making the need for SFT immediately intuitive
when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
suggested_caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields
teaching_value: Illustrates that human feedback quality depends on who the labelers are, prompting students to think critically about whose values and preferences are encoded in RLHF-trained models and the subjectivity of 'good' responses
when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
suggested_caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases
teaching_value: Clarifies the architectural and conceptual shift from imitation learning in SFT to reward-maximization in RLHF, helping students understand why a reward model is needed and how the training loop changes
when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
suggested_caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective
teaching_value: Translates the mathematical loss function into an intuitive visual, helping students understand that the RM is trained to maximize the score gap between preferred and rejected responses rather than predict absolute scores
when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with calculus/ML background who benefit from seeing loss landscape shapes
suggested_caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A screenshot of the actual web UI used by OpenAI labelers to rank InstructGPT responses, showing a prompt, multiple model-generated responses, a 1–7 rating scale, and a ranking interface
teaching_value: Makes the abstract data collection process tangible by showing the real tool used; helps students understand the practical mechanics of comparison data collection, the dual rating+ranking design, and the inter-labeler agreement challenge
when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic and what it implies about subjectivity in human feedback
suggested_caption: OpenAI's labeler UI: rank responses 1–7 and by preference to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) pretraining, (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and (3) RLHF with a reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.
teaching_value: Gives students a high-level mental model of the full RLHF training pipeline before diving into details, making it easier to understand where each component fits and why the KL divergence constraint is needed to prevent the RL phase from drifting too far from the SFT model.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map, and revisit when explaining why the RL phase needs a KL penalty to stay close to the SFT checkpoint.
suggested_caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (huyenchip.com)

description: The mathematical objective function used in RLHF, showing the combined loss as a sum of the reward model score expectation and a KL divergence penalty term that constrains the RL-tuned policy to stay close to the SFT model.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract idea of 'don't stray too far from SFT' concrete and precise; students can see exactly how the KL divergence term is weighted and summed with the reward signal, bridging intuition and formal optimization.
when_to_show: Introduce after students understand the three-phase pipeline; use it when explaining why unconstrained reward maximization leads to reward hacking and how the KL term prevents degenerate outputs.
suggested_caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT. (huyenchip.com)

description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs 'know what they know' and that RL-based methods could force models to only assert information they are confident about.
teaching_value: Presents a specific researcher's hypothesis and proposed interventions, helping students critically evaluate the claim that RLHF should reduce hallucination — especially useful when contrasted with the empirical finding that RLHF actually worsened hallucination.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the gap between theoretical expectations and empirical results of RLHF; use it to prompt critical thinking about whether RL can reliably teach models to express uncertainty.
suggested_caption: Schulman's proposed RL-based fixes for LLM hallucination. (huyenchip.com)

description: A bar chart or comparison graphic from the InstructGPT paper showing that RLHF-trained models exhibit higher hallucination rates than SFT-only models, despite being preferred overall by human labelers across other quality dimensions.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that contradicts the intuitive expectation that RLHF improves all aspects of model quality; teaches students to distinguish between aggregate human preference and specific failure modes, and illustrates the trade-off between helpfulness and factual accuracy.
when_to_show: Show after introducing RLHF benefits to immediately introduce nuance; ideal for discussions on evaluation metrics, reward hacking, and why optimizing for human preference does not guarantee factual correctness.
suggested_caption: InstructGPT data: RLHF increases hallucination despite higher overall preference. (huyenchip.com)

description: A horizontal pipeline diagram showing the full LLM development and training workflow, from data collection through pre-training, continued pre-training, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and alignment/post-training stages, with annotations highlighting new methodologies covered in the article.
teaching_value: Gives students a bird's-eye map of the entire LLM training lifecycle so they can situate individual techniques (e.g., DPO, continued pre-training) within the broader workflow before diving into details.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM training to orient students before introducing specific stages; return to it as a navigation aid when transitioning between pre-training and post-training topics.
suggested_caption: Full LLM training pipeline: pre-training through post-training alignment. (Raschka)

description: A bar chart comparing MMLU benchmark scores across the latest open-weight LLMs (including Qwen 2 variants and contemporaries), with model names on the x-axis and MMLU accuracy scores on the y-axis, sourced directly from official research papers.
teaching_value: Provides concrete empirical evidence for how different model sizes and architectures compare on a standard reasoning benchmark, teaching students to interpret benchmark comparisons and understand the performance landscape of open-weight models.
when_to_show: Surface when discussing how to evaluate LLMs or when comparing Qwen 2 to other models; useful for teaching students to critically read benchmark tables and understand what MMLU measures.
suggested_caption: MMLU scores for recent open-weight LLMs; higher is better. (Raschka)

description: A structured summary table or diagram listing the key techniques used in Qwen 2 pre-training, including data filtering, data mixing, synthetic data generation via prior Qwen models, multi-task instruction data inclusion, and a two-stage pre-training approach (standard pre-training followed by long-context continued pre-training).
teaching_value: Consolidates multiple pre-training design decisions into a single reference, helping students understand that modern pre-training is not a single step but a deliberate combination of data curation, synthesis, and staged training strategies.
when_to_show: Use when teaching data-centric approaches to pre-training or when contrasting Qwen 2's pipeline with other models; ideal for a discussion on why data quality matters as much as quantity.
suggested_caption: Qwen 2 pre-training techniques including two-stage continued pre-training. (Raschka)

description: A flowchart illustrating the typical LLM development flow: starting from a base pre-trained model, moving through supervised fine-tuning (SFT), then alignment via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or direct preference optimization (DPO), resulting in a chat/instruction-following model.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the sequential relationship between pre-training, SFT, and alignment, helping students understand why each stage is necessary and how they build on one another to produce a usable assistant model.
when_to_show: Introduce before explaining DPO or SFT in detail; also effective when students ask 'why can't you just use the base model?' to show the role of post-training stages.
suggested_caption: Typical LLM development flow: pre-training → SFT → alignment. (Raschka)

description: A summary diagram or table of Qwen 2 post-training techniques, covering the two-phase alignment approach: offline DPO on an existing dataset followed by online DPO with a reward model using rejection sampling, plus details on dataset construction combining human labeling and synthetic annotation.
teaching_value: Illustrates the distinction between offline and online preference optimization, and shows how rejection sampling integrates into training, giving students a concrete example of how alignment is implemented in a real production model.
when_to_show: Use when teaching the difference between offline and online RL/DPO methods, or when discussing how preference datasets are constructed; pairs well with the DPO overview diagram (Image 9).
suggested_caption: Qwen 2 post-training: offline then online DPO with rejection sampling. (Raschka)

description: A detailed overview diagram of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for LLM alignment, likely showing the training objective, the role of chosen vs. rejected responses, the reference model, and how the policy model is updated to prefer human-preferred outputs over rejected ones.
teaching_value: Provides a visual explanation of the DPO algorithm's mechanics, making the abstract mathematical objective concrete by showing the flow of data (prompt, chosen response, rejected response) through the training process.
when_to_show: Show when first introducing DPO to students, especially before or after a mathematical derivation; also useful as a reference when students encounter DPO mentioned in multiple model papers throughout the article.
suggested_caption: DPO alignment: training LLMs to prefer chosen over rejected responses. (Raschka)

description: A three-step pre-training pipeline diagram for Apple's AFM (Apple Foundation Models), showing: (1) core pre-training on 6.3 trillion tokens, (2) continued pre-training with down-weighted web-crawl data and up-weighted math/code data, and (3) a final context-extension or specialization stage.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how a major industry lab structures multi-stage pre-training with deliberate data curriculum changes between stages, teaching students that pre-training is not monolithic and that data weighting strategies evolve across stages.
when_to_show: Use when comparing pre-training strategies across different organizations, or when teaching the concept of training curricula and how data composition shifts between pre-training stages.
suggested_caption: Apple AFM's 3-stage pre-training pipeline with data reweighting. (Raschka)

description: Diagram comparing two training approaches: (a) standard pre-training using cross-entropy loss on training tokens, and (b) knowledge distillation where a 3B student model learns from both training tokens and soft labels from a 6.4B teacher model, with a convex combination loss (0.9 weight on teacher labels).
teaching_value: Visually contrasts standard pre-training loss with distillation loss, making the mechanism of knowledge transfer from teacher to student model concrete and easy to compare side-by-side.
when_to_show: Show when introducing knowledge distillation — use it to explain why distillation provides a richer training signal than standard cross-entropy on hard labels alone.
suggested_caption: Knowledge distillation: student (3B) learns from teacher (6.4B) soft labels. (Raschka)

description: Summary table or diagram listing the techniques used across the Apple Foundation Model (AFM) pre-training pipeline, including core pre-training, continued pre-training, and context lengthening stages.
teaching_value: Provides a consolidated reference for the multi-stage AFM pre-training process, helping students see how different techniques (distillation, data mixing, context extension) fit together in a real production pipeline.
when_to_show: Show as a wrap-up after covering each AFM pre-training stage, or as an overview before diving into individual stages to orient students.
suggested_caption: AFM pre-training pipeline: core training, distillation, and context lengthening. (Raschka)

description: Summary diagram of the Apple Foundation Model (AFM) post-training pipeline, covering supervised fine-tuning (SFT), rejection sampling, preference tuning methods (DPO, IPO, online RL), and the iTeC committee-based iterative approach with a reward model.
teaching_value: Illustrates how multiple post-training alignment techniques are combined in a real system, showing students that production models use hybrid approaches rather than a single algorithm.
when_to_show: Show when teaching post-training alignment, especially to contrast single-algorithm approaches with Apple's committee-based iterative method.
suggested_caption: AFM post-training: iTeC combines SFT, DPO, IPO, and online RL. (Raschka)

description: Summary diagram of Gemma 2 pre-training techniques, highlighting knowledge distillation for smaller models (27B trained from scratch; smaller models distilled), training data scale, and the rationale that small models are often undertrained.
teaching_value: Allows students to compare Gemma 2's pre-training strategy with Apple's AFM, reinforcing the pattern of using knowledge distillation for smaller models as an industry-wide trend.
when_to_show: Show when comparing pre-training strategies across model families, or when discussing why knowledge distillation is preferred over simply scaling data for smaller models.
suggested_caption: Gemma 2 pre-training: distillation for small models, scratch training for 27B. (Raschka)

description: Summary diagram of Gemma 2 post-training techniques, including SFT with teacher-generated responses (knowledge distillation during fine-tuning), RLHF with a reward model 10x larger than the policy model, and WARP model averaging.
teaching_value: Demonstrates an advanced RLHF setup where the reward model is disproportionately large, and shows how knowledge distillation extends into the post-training phase — a non-obvious design choice students can analyze.
when_to_show: Show when discussing RLHF reward model design choices, or when contrasting Gemma 2's post-training with Apple's iTeC approach.
suggested_caption: Gemma 2 post-training: oversized reward model and WARP averaging. (Raschka)

description: Bar chart showing MMLU benchmark scores for multiple LLM models including Llama 3.1 variants (8B, 70B, 405B), GPT-4, and others, illustrating how Llama 3.1 405B approaches or matches frontier model performance.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete quantitative benchmark to understand where Llama 3.1 sits relative to other models, grounding abstract architectural discussions in measurable performance outcomes.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Llama 3.1 to motivate why its training approach matters, or when teaching how benchmark results relate to model size and training choices.
suggested_caption: MMLU scores: Llama 3.1 405B approaches GPT-4 level performance. (Raschka)

description: Summary diagram of Llama 3.1 pre-training pipeline across three stages: initial pre-training on 15T tokens, continued pre-training with adjusted data mix (~800B tokens), and annealing on high-quality data (including GSM8K and MATH) for the final stage.
teaching_value: Shows students how a 3-stage pre-training curriculum works in practice, with the annealing stage demonstrating that targeted high-quality data at the end of training can significantly boost benchmark performance.
when_to_show: Show when teaching data curriculum strategies in pre-training, especially to illustrate the outsized impact of final-stage annealing on specific benchmarks.
suggested_caption: Llama 3.1 pre-training: 3 stages ending with high-quality annealing data. (Raschka)

description: Annotated figure from the Llama 3.1 technical paper showing the iterative post-training procedure: multiple rounds of SFT and DPO, rejection sampling using a reward model, and incorporation of both human and synthetic data across rounds.
teaching_value: Provides a real paper figure with author annotations, helping students read primary sources while understanding how iterative SFT+DPO rounds with rejection sampling work as an alternative to PPO-based RLHF.
when_to_show: Show when teaching DPO-based alignment pipelines, especially to contrast with PPO/RLHF and to demonstrate why iterative rounds improve stability.
suggested_caption: Llama 3.1 post-training: iterative SFT+DPO rounds with rejection sampling. (Raschka/Meta)

description: Summary diagram of Llama 3.1 post-training techniques, covering SFT, rejection sampling, DPO, reward model training, and model averaging/merging applied to SFT, DPO, and reward models across training rounds.
teaching_value: Consolidates the full Llama 3.1 post-training pipeline into one reference, highlighting model averaging as a stabilization technique — a detail students might miss from reading the paper alone.
when_to_show: Show as a summary after covering Llama 3.1 post-training details, or to compare with AFM and Gemma 2 post-training approaches side by side.
suggested_caption: Llama 3.1 post-training: SFT, DPO, rejection sampling, and model averaging. (Raschka)

description: A comparison table or visual overview showing pre-training techniques used across four LLMs: Qwen 2, Apple AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3, highlighting shared and distinct methods such as multi-stage pre-training, context lengthening, and annealing steps.
teaching_value: Allows students to directly compare pre-training pipeline choices across major LLMs, making abstract methodological differences concrete and memorable through side-by-side visual structure.
when_to_show: Show when teaching the pre-training phase of LLM development, especially when contrasting how different organizations structure their training pipelines and what steps they share.
suggested_caption: Pre-training technique comparison across Qwen 2, AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3. (Raschka)

description: A comparison table or visual overview showing post-training techniques used across four LLMs: Qwen 2, Apple AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3, covering methods such as rejection sampling, DPO, RLHF, and supervised fine-tuning stages.
teaching_value: Helps students see that there is no single dominant post-training recipe, and concretely illustrates where consensus exists (rejection sampling) versus where approaches diverge (DPO vs. RLHF), reinforcing nuanced understanding of alignment techniques.
when_to_show: Show when discussing LLM alignment and post-training strategies, particularly to illustrate the diversity of approaches and prompt discussion about trade-offs between DPO and RLHF.
suggested_caption: Post-training technique comparison across Qwen 2, AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3. (Raschka)

description: Diagram illustrating the attention mechanism added to a recurrent neural network (RNN) for sequence-to-sequence tasks, showing how attention weights connect encoder hidden states to decoder outputs for neural machine translation.
teaching_value: Helps students visualize how attention allows an RNN decoder to selectively focus on different parts of the input sequence, solving the bottleneck of fixed-length context vectors and motivating the later transformer architecture.
when_to_show: Show when introducing attention mechanisms before covering transformers; use to contrast RNN-based attention with the self-attention in transformers.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau et al. (2014): Attention mechanism added to RNN encoder-decoder.

description: Architecture diagram of the original Transformer model from 'Attention Is All You Need', showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right) with multi-head attention blocks, feed-forward layers, positional encoding, and residual connections.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete visual reference for the full transformer architecture, enabling students to identify and locate key components (multi-head attention, positional encoding, layer normalization) and understand how encoder and decoder interact.
when_to_show: Show as the central reference diagram when teaching the transformer architecture; revisit when discussing BERT (encoder-only) and GPT (decoder-only) as architectural derivatives.
suggested_caption: Vaswani et al. (2017): Original Transformer encoder-decoder architecture diagram.

description: Diagram from the BERT paper illustrating pre-training objectives (Masked Language Modeling and Next Sentence Prediction) and fine-tuning on downstream tasks, showing bidirectional context flow through the transformer encoder.
teaching_value: Clarifies how BERT's masked language modeling enables bidirectional context learning, and how a single pretrained model can be fine-tuned for multiple NLP tasks, illustrating the pretrain-then-finetune paradigm.
when_to_show: Show when explaining encoder-style LLMs and the bifurcation between encoder (BERT) and decoder (GPT) architectures; use to contrast with GPT's unidirectional pretraining.
suggested_caption: Devlin et al. (2018): BERT pre-training with masked language modeling and fine-tuning.

description: Diagram from the original GPT paper showing the decoder-only transformer architecture and the two-stage approach: unsupervised pretraining via next-word prediction followed by supervised fine-tuning on downstream tasks.
teaching_value: Illustrates the autoregressive, unidirectional nature of GPT and the pretraining-finetuning pipeline that underpins modern LLMs like ChatGPT, helping students contrast it with BERT's bidirectional approach.
when_to_show: Show alongside the BERT diagram when teaching the encoder vs. decoder architectural split; use as a foundation before introducing GPT-2, GPT-3, and InstructGPT.
suggested_caption: Radford & Narasimhan (2018): GPT decoder-only architecture with pretraining and fine-tuning.

description: Diagram from the BART paper comparing BART's encoder-decoder denoising pretraining with other models (BERT, GPT, etc.), showing how corrupted input text is reconstructed by the full sequence-to-sequence architecture.
teaching_value: Shows students how combining encoder and decoder components enables a model to excel at both understanding and generation tasks, and illustrates denoising as a pretraining objective distinct from masked LM or next-word prediction.
when_to_show: Show after covering BERT and GPT to illustrate how BART synthesizes both paradigms; useful when discussing trade-offs between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures.
suggested_caption: Lewis et al. (2019): BART combines encoder-decoder for denoising sequence-to-sequence pretraining.

description: Diagram from the FlashAttention paper illustrating the IO-aware tiled computation approach for computing exact attention efficiently, contrasting memory access patterns between standard attention and FlashAttention on GPU hardware.
teaching_value: Helps students understand that attention's computational bottleneck is often memory bandwidth rather than raw FLOPs, and how tiling and kernel fusion reduce HBM reads/writes to achieve significant speedups.
when_to_show: Show when discussing efficiency improvements to the transformer; use to motivate why hardware-aware algorithm design matters for scaling LLMs.
suggested_caption: Dao et al. (2022): FlashAttention uses IO-aware tiling for fast, memory-efficient attention.

description: Results figure from the Cramming paper showing performance (e.g., GLUE scores) of a BERT-style model trained on a single GPU for 24 hours, compared against baselines, with analysis of training efficiency trade-offs.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that model size and training efficiency interact non-linearly — larger models learn more efficiently per token even if throughput is lower — challenging the assumption that smaller models are always preferable under compute constraints.
when_to_show: Show when discussing scaling laws and compute budgets; use to challenge students' intuitions about the relationship between model size, training time, and performance.
suggested_caption: Geiping & Goldstein (2022): BERT trained on one GPU in 24h reveals efficiency trade-offs.

description: Taxonomy diagram from the PEFT survey categorizing over 40 parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods into groups such as prefix tuning, adapters, and low-rank adaptation (LoRA), with comparisons of their properties.
teaching_value: Gives students a structured overview of the PEFT landscape, enabling them to compare methods along dimensions like parameter count, task performance, and computational cost, and understand why full fine-tuning is often impractical for large models.
when_to_show: Show when introducing fine-tuning strategies for LLMs; use as a reference map when students encounter specific PEFT methods like LoRA or adapters in later readings.
suggested_caption: Lialin et al. (2022): Taxonomy of 40+ parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.

description: Figures from the Chinchilla paper showing the relationship between model size, number of training tokens, and loss, illustrating the linear scaling law and demonstrating that Chinchilla (70B parameters, 1.4T tokens) outperforms GPT-3 (175B parameters, 300B tokens).
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the Chinchilla scaling law — that training tokens and model parameters must scale proportionally — correcting the misconception that larger models always win and showing that data quantity is equally critical.
when_to_show: Show when teaching scaling laws; use to prompt discussion on why many contemporary LLMs were undertrained and how this insight changed subsequent model development.
suggested_caption: Hoffmann et al. (2022): Chinchilla scaling law — training tokens matter as much as model size.

description: Three-step pipeline diagram from the InstructGPT paper showing: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human-written prompt-response pairs, (2) reward model training from human preference rankings, and (3) PPO-based reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align GPT-3.
teaching_value: Provides a clear visual breakdown of the RLHF pipeline, helping students understand how human preferences are encoded into a reward model and used to steer LLM behavior, which is the core mechanism behind ChatGPT.
when_to_show: Show when introducing LLM alignment and RLHF; use as the primary reference diagram before discussing Constitutional AI and other alignment approaches.
suggested_caption: Ouyang et al. (2022): InstructGPT three-step RLHF pipeline for aligning GPT-3.

description: Diagram illustrating the Constitutional AI training pipeline, showing how a set of written principles (a 'constitution') guides a self-critique and revision loop to produce a harmless AI system without direct human supervision at each step.
teaching_value: Helps students visualize how Constitutional AI replaces human labelers with an AI-driven self-improvement loop based on explicit principles, clarifying the distinction between RLHF and Constitutional AI alignment approaches.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Constitutional AI as an alternative to RLHF, specifically to contrast human-feedback-based alignment with principle-based AI self-supervision.
suggested_caption: Constitutional AI: self-supervised harmlessness training via written principles. (Anthropic, 2022)

description: Wide-format diagram depicting the three-step RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) procedure: (1) supervised fine-tuning on demonstrations, (2) training a reward model from human preference comparisons, and (3) optimizing the language model policy using PPO against the reward model.
teaching_value: Provides a clear visual walkthrough of the canonical RLHF pipeline, allowing students to map each abstract step (SFT, reward modeling, RL optimization) to a concrete stage, and understand why PPO outperforms pure supervised learning for alignment.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on RLHF or instruction-tuning; use as a reference diagram when explaining InstructGPT or comparing alignment techniques.
suggested_caption: The three-step RLHF pipeline: SFT, reward modeling, and PPO optimization. (Stiennon et al., 2022)

description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel attention heads, including linear projections, scaled dot-product attention blocks, concatenation, and final linear transformation
teaching_value: Visually decomposes the multi-head attention mechanism into its sequential steps, helping students understand how inputs are split into subspaces, processed in parallel, and recombined
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining how multi-head attention differs from single-head attention; use to walk through each computational step before presenting the mathematical formulation
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention mechanism. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) with multi-head attention and feed-forward layers, and the decoder stack (right) with masked multi-head attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward layers, plus input/output embeddings and positional encoding
teaching_value: Provides a complete structural overview of the Transformer, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact, where positional encoding is added, and how information flows from input tokens to output predictions
when_to_show: Show early when introducing the Transformer architecture as a whole; reference back when discussing individual components like attention or positional encoding to anchor them in the full model
suggested_caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating wave patterns across positions and dimensions with values ranging from -1 (black) to 1 (white)
teaching_value: Makes the abstract sinusoidal encoding formula concrete by showing how different frequency sinusoids create unique positional signatures, helping students understand why each position gets a distinct encoding
when_to_show: Show after introducing the need for positional encoding due to permutation invariance of self-attention; use to connect the mathematical formula to its visual pattern before discussing learned alternatives
suggested_caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap, L=32, d=128. (Weng, 2023)

description: Diagram illustrating Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) showing how 2D rotation matrices are applied to query and key vectors in pairs of dimensions, with rotation angles proportional to position index i and dimension-specific frequencies
teaching_value: Clarifies the geometric intuition behind RoPE by showing how position information is encoded as rotations in 2D subspaces, helping students understand why relative positions are naturally captured in dot products
when_to_show: Introduce after covering sinusoidal encoding; show when explaining how RoPE encodes relative positions and why it improves context length extrapolation compared to absolute positional encodings
suggested_caption: Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) applied to query/key vectors. (Su et al., 2021)

description: Side-by-side comparison of training in vanilla Transformer vs Transformer-XL with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches hidden states from previous segments and extends the effective attention span across segment boundaries
teaching_value: Directly illustrates the key innovation of Transformer-XL — segment-level recurrence — by contrasting it with the fixed-context limitation of vanilla Transformer, making the concept of extended attention span tangible
when_to_show: Show when introducing the context segmentation problem of vanilla Transformers; use the side-by-side comparison to motivate why Transformer-XL's recurrence mechanism is needed
suggested_caption: Vanilla Transformer vs Transformer-XL training with segment recurrence. (Dai et al., 2019)

description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory tiers: a standard memory buffer of recent past activations and a compressed memory buffer of older activations, with compression functions reducing older memories to save space while retaining information
teaching_value: Illustrates the two-tier memory hierarchy of the Compressive Transformer, helping students understand how compression extends temporal range beyond Transformer-XL while managing memory costs
when_to_show: Introduce after Transformer-XL as a natural extension; use to explain the tradeoff between temporal range and memory cost, and to motivate the compression loss objectives
suggested_caption: Compressive Transformer with memory and compressed memory tiers. (Rae et al., 2019)

description: Bar chart showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) across memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right), demonstrating that compressed memories (older positions) receive comparable attention to recent memories
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that compressed memories are still attended to meaningfully, validating the Compressive Transformer's design and helping students understand that older context is not simply discarded
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Compressive Transformer architecture; use as empirical support that compression preserves useful information and that the model attends to both recent and older compressed memories
suggested_caption: Attention weights across memory positions in Compressive Transformer. (Rae et al., 2019)

description: Diagram of SPALM (Sparse Pointer Attention Language Model) showing how short-term memory (past hidden states) and long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore) are combined via a gating mechanism to produce context-aware token representations
teaching_value: Illustrates the dual-memory architecture of SPALM, helping students understand how differentiable short-term recurrence and non-differentiable long-term retrieval can be integrated through learned gating
when_to_show: Introduce when discussing non-differentiable external memory augmentation; use to contrast with purely recurrent approaches and to explain how retrieval-augmented language models work
suggested_caption: SPALM combining short-term hidden states with long-term k-NN memory. (Yogatama et al., 2021)

description: Line graph comparing perplexity vs training steps for: training a memorizing transformer from scratch, fine-tuning a vanilla Transformer with k-NN memory added, and a baseline vanilla Transformer, showing fine-tuning achieves similar performance to training from scratch
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical efficiency of the Memorizing Transformer approach — that adding k-NN memory via fine-tuning is as effective as full training — helping students understand the modularity and transferability of memory augmentation
when_to_show: Show when discussing the Memorizing Transformer to highlight its practical advantage; use to motivate why fine-tuning with memory augmentation is a viable and efficient strategy
suggested_caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches training Memorizing Transformer from scratch. (Wu et al., 2022)

description: Diagram showing how ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) modifies attention scores by subtracting a linear positional bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions, with different slopes per attention head
teaching_value: Concretely shows how ALiBi injects relative position information directly into attention scores without learned embeddings, helping students understand the simplicity and extrapolation benefits of bias-based positional encoding
when_to_show: Introduce when comparing positional encoding strategies; use to contrast with RoPE and sinusoidal encoding, emphasizing ALiBi's simplicity and its ability to generalize to longer sequences at inference
suggested_caption: ALiBi adds linear distance-based bias to attention scores per head. (Press et al., 2021)

description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary, T5 relative, ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve as context length grows beyond training length.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the extrapolation problem in positional encodings — students can directly compare how different schemes degrade when inference context exceeds training context, making ALiBi's advantage concrete and measurable.
when_to_show: Show when introducing ALiBi or comparing positional encoding schemes; use to motivate why additive linear biases outperform learned or sinusoidal encodings for out-of-distribution sequence lengths.
suggested_caption: ALiBi extrapolates better than sinusoidal, rotary, and T5 encodings. (Press et al. 2021)

description: Diagram showing the Universal Transformer's recurrent refinement loop: a sequence of token representations is updated repeatedly across multiple time steps in parallel, with each step applying self-attention and a transition function to all positions simultaneously.
teaching_value: Clarifies the key architectural innovation of Universal Transformer — that depth is dynamic and recurrent rather than fixed, helping students distinguish it from standard Transformers and understand how RNN-style recurrence is integrated.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer to illustrate how recurrence replaces fixed-depth stacking; contrast with standard Transformer layer diagram to highlight the iterative refinement concept.
suggested_caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al. 2019)

description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.
teaching_value: Shows students how the Universal Transformer adapts the standard encoder-decoder architecture by sharing recurrent weights, and highlights the cross-attention connection from decoder to encoder's final state.
when_to_show: Show after the loop diagram (Image 2) to give the full encoder-decoder picture; useful when comparing Universal Transformer to standard seq2seq Transformers.
suggested_caption: Universal Transformer encoder-decoder with shared recurrent weights and cross-attention. (Dehghani et al. 2019)

description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z used in adaptive attention span, showing how it maps query-key distance to a [0,1] value with a smooth sigmoid-shaped transition controlled by parameter z, effectively creating a soft boundary for the attention window.
teaching_value: Makes the mathematical definition of the soft mask concrete — students can see how z shifts the transition point and how the function smoothly interpolates between full attention and no attention, enabling gradient-based learning of span length.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the m_z formula to help students visualize what 'soft' means in this context and how z controls the effective span boundary.
suggested_caption: Soft mask function controls adaptive attention span boundary via learnable parameter z. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2019)

description: Illustration of three depth-adaptive classifier configurations: (1) sequence-specific — one exit shared by all tokens in a sequence; (2) token-specific — each token has its own exit; (3) a hybrid approach. Diagrams show how classifiers attach to different transformer layers.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the design space of early-exit strategies in transformers — the visual contrast between sequence-level and token-level granularity makes the trade-off between efficiency and flexibility immediately apparent.
when_to_show: Show when teaching adaptive depth or conditional computation; use to compare granularity levels before discussing CALM, and to motivate why token-level exits are more flexible but harder to train.
suggested_caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-specific, token-specific, and hybrid. (Elabyad et al. 2020)

description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image generation in Image Transformer. Each shows a query block (black outline) and the corresponding attention span (cyan outline) for a query pixel q, illustrating how context is restricted to a local neighborhood.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how local attention windows are defined differently for 1D (raster-scan linearized) vs 2D (spatial grid) image representations, helping students understand the trade-off between computational cost and spatial locality.
when_to_show: Show when introducing local/fixed-context attention mechanisms, especially in the context of image generation; use to contrast with full self-attention before discussing efficiency gains.
suggested_caption: 1D vs 2D local attention spans for pixel query q in Image Transformer. (Parmer et al. 2018)

description: Two-row figure comparing attention connectivity for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention. Top row shows which tokens attend to which in a sequence diagram; bottom row shows the corresponding sparse attention matrices.
teaching_value: Directly visualizes the sparsity patterns that make Sparse Transformer efficient — students can see exactly which connections are retained vs. dropped and how strided vs. fixed patterns differ, linking the abstract math to concrete matrix structure.
when_to_show: Show when introducing sparse attention mechanisms; use the matrix view to quantify sparsity and the connectivity diagram to explain how information still flows between distant tokens through multi-hop paths.
suggested_caption: Full vs. strided vs. fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al. 2019)

description: Side-by-side attention pattern diagrams for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird, each showing how global tokens, local sliding-window attention, and random attention are combined. Color-coded blocks indicate different attention types (global, local, random).
teaching_value: Enables direct visual comparison of three influential efficient attention architectures, making it easy to see what each adds (e.g., Big Bird adds random attention on top of Longformer's local+global) and how they relate to each other.
when_to_show: Show when comparing efficient long-sequence transformer architectures; use as a summary diagram after covering each model individually to reinforce similarities and differences.
suggested_caption: Attention patterns compared: ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird global-local combinations.

description: Visualization of the LSH attention matrix after bucketing and sorting: tokens are grouped into hash buckets (shown by colored blocks along the diagonal), and attention is only computed within each bucket, producing a block-diagonal sparse attention matrix.
teaching_value: Shows the resulting sparse structure of LSH attention after hashing, helping students understand why only within-bucket attention is computed and how the block-diagonal pattern approximates full attention for the most relevant token pairs.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (Image 10) to illustrate the resulting attention matrix structure; use to connect the hashing algorithm to the actual computation savings.
suggested_caption: LSH attention produces block-diagonal sparse matrix by attending within hash buckets. (Kitaev et al. 2020)

description: Step-by-step diagram of the four LSH attention stages: (1) bucketing tokens by hash, (2) sorting tokens by bucket assignment, (3) chunking sorted tokens into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention only within each chunk. Uses color coding to track token assignments.
teaching_value: Breaks down the LSH attention algorithm into discrete, visually trackable steps, allowing students to follow how a query token ends up attending only to its nearest neighbors without exhaustive pairwise comparison.
when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; walk through each of the four steps sequentially before showing the resulting attention matrix (Image 9).
suggested_caption: Four steps of LSH attention: bucket, sort, chunk, then attend within chunks. (Kitaev et al. 2020)

description: Two-panel figure: (Left) architecture diagram of Linformer showing linear projection layers E and F added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k; (Right) line plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer versus standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how low-rank projection reduces the quadratic attention bottleneck to linear complexity, and the right panel provides empirical evidence of the computational speedup at scale — making the theoretical claim concrete.
when_to_show: Show when introducing efficient attention mechanisms, specifically after explaining why standard self-attention is O(L²) and before deriving the Linformer projection math.
suggested_caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear inference time. (Wang et al. 2020)

description: Two-panel diagram contrasting computation order: (Left) standard softmax attention computing the full L×L attention matrix before multiplying by values; (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) reordering operations so the kernel feature map φ is applied first, enabling linear-time computation by avoiding the explicit attention matrix.
teaching_value: Illustrates the key algorithmic insight of RFA — that by reordering matrix multiplications using the associative property, the expensive O(L²) attention matrix is never materialized, making the computational savings immediately intuitive.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how kernel-based methods approximate softmax attention, specifically to illustrate why reordering (φ(Q)(φ(K)ᵀV)) is cheaper than ((QKᵀ)V).
suggested_caption: RFA reorders computation to avoid the full attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)

description: Two-panel comparison of approximation error plots for the Performer model: (Left) i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features showing orthogonal features have lower approximation error; (Right) sin/cos random features vs. positive random features showing positive features yield lower error for softmax kernel approximation.
teaching_value: Provides empirical justification for two key design choices in Performer (FAVOR+): using orthogonal rather than i.i.d. features, and using positive rather than trigonometric features — helping students understand why these choices matter quantitatively.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the Performer's FAVOR+ mechanism, when discussing why orthogonal and positive random features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos alternatives.
suggested_caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer. (Choromanski et al. 2021)

description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) standard Transformer-XL with layer norm after residual addition; (2) Transformer-XL with layer norm reordered to before the sub-layer (pre-norm); (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using pre-norm, designed for stable RL training.
teaching_value: Clearly shows the two architectural modifications (layer norm reordering and gating units) that stabilize Transformer training in RL settings, allowing students to visually diff the three architectures and understand each change's role.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Transformers for RL, specifically to explain why vanilla Transformer-XL is unstable in RL and how GTrXL's two modifications address this.
suggested_caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm for stable RL training. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
